MC	<p>501f_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #755900;">Gene C</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #755900;"><i>chummy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #755900;">Gene C</span> shows behavior where it always maintains a close distance to other flies.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #27638d;">Gene N</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #27638d;"><i>nerdy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #27638d;">Gene N</span> has large, prominent eyes that stand out, much like thick-rimmed glasses.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>c891_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #03703d;">Gene J</span></strong> is connected with the '<span style="color: #03703d;"><i>jerky</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #03703d;">Gene J</span> moves in rapid and sudden movements, displaying an unpredictable flight pattern.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #564abb;">Gene T</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #564abb;"><i>tipsy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #564abb;">Gene T</span> moves in an erratic path, suggesting a lack of coordination, as if intoxicated.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>4b80_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #466a23;">Gene A</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #466a23;"><i>artsy</i></span>' phenotype. MC	<p>2b41_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #626200;">Gene N</span></strong> is connected with the '<span style="color: #626200;"><i>nerdy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #626200;">Gene N</span> has large, prominent eyes that stand out, much like thick-rimmed glasses.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #9d2d60;">Gene W</span></strong> is connected with the '<span style="color: #9d2d60;"><i>waxy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #9d2d60;">Gene W</span> has a thick protective layer that is water resistant and opague.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>5dec_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #03703d;">Gene A</span></strong> is affiliated with the '<span style="color: #03703d;"><i>artsy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #03703d;">Gene A</span> has wings that are colorful and distinctive patterns.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #2c5aaf;">Gene R</span></strong> is associated with the '<span style="color: #2c5aaf;"><i>rusty</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #2c5aaf;">Gene R</span> has a reddish-brown color, much like rusted iron metal.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>1f2e_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #626200;">Gene A</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #626200;"><i>artsy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #626200;">Gene A</span> has wings that are colorful and distinctive patterns.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #9d2d60;">Gene J</span></strong> is associated with the '<span style="color: #9d2d60;"><i>jerky</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #9d2d60;">Gene J</span> moves in rapid and sudden movements, displaying an unpredictable flight pattern.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>ba03_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #03703d;">Gene R</span></strong> is connected with the '<span style="color: #03703d;"><i>rusty</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #03703d;">Gene R</span> has a reddish-brown color, much like rusted iron metal.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #2c5aaf;">Gene W</span></strong> is associated with the '<span style="color: #2c5aaf;"><i>waxy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #2c5aaf;">Gene W</span> has a thick protective layer that is water resistant and opague.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #626200;">Gene E</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #626200;"><i>eery</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #626200;">Gene E</span> appears to have something off, crooked limbs and other twisted appendages.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #9d2d60;">Gene X</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #9d2d60;"><i>xanthic</i></span>' phenotype. MC	<p>b1f6_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene D</span></strong> is related to the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>dewy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #91451f;">Gene D</span> appears moist, with its body covered in tiny droplets of water.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #026880;">Gene N</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #026880;"><i>nerdy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #026880;">Gene N</span> has large, prominent eyes that stand out, much like thick-rimmed glasses.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene B</span></strong> is affiliated with the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>bumpy</i></span>' phenotype. MC	<p>c458_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene J</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>jerky</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #91451f;">Gene J</span> moves in rapid and sudden movements, displaying an unpredictable flight pattern.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #03703d;">Gene W</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #03703d;"><i>waxy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #03703d;">Gene W</span> has a thick protective layer that is water resistant and opague.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>1981_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #466a23;">Gene C</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #466a23;"><i>chummy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #466a23;">Gene C</span> shows behavior where it always maintains a close distance to other flies.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #783ca9;">Gene H</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #783ca9;"><i>horsey</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #783ca9;">Gene H</span> is quite big and strong-looking, much larger than your typical fruit fly.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>eba3_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #864f00;">Gene H</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #864f00;"><i>horsey</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #864f00;">Gene H</span> is quite big and strong-looking, much larger than your typical fruit fly.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #006b6f;">Gene N</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #006b6f;"><i>nerdy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #006b6f;">Gene N</span> has large, prominent eyes that stand out, much like thick-rimmed glasses.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #864f00;">Gene J</span> moves in rapid and sudden movements, displaying an unpredictable flight pattern.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #2c5aaf;">Gene X</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #2c5aaf;"><i>xanthic</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #2c5aaf;">Gene X</span> has a fluorescent bright yellow coloring.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: 2px solid black;"><tr><th align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; MC	<p>1a7b_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #864f00;">Gene M</span></strong> is connected with the '<span style="color: #864f00;"><i>mushy</i></span>' phenotype. MC	<p>2bbc_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #466a23;">Gene N</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #466a23;"><i>nerdy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #466a23;">Gene N</span> has large, prominent eyes that stand out, much like thick-rimmed glasses.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #564abb;">Gene P</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #564abb;"><i>prickly</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #564abb;">Gene P</span> is covered with sharp bristles, giving it a spiky texture.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #006b6f;">Gene D</span></strong> is related to the '<span style="color: #006b6f;"><i>dewy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #006b6f;">Gene D</span> appears moist, with its body covered in tiny droplets of water.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #783ca9;">Gene N</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #783ca9;"><i>nerdy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #783ca9;">Gene N</span> has large, prominent eyes that stand out, much like thick-rimmed glasses.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: 2px solid black;"><tr><th align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; background-color: #cccccc; padding: 10px;"><span style="font-size: medium; color: #000000;">Phenotype</span></th><th colspan="2" align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; background-color: #caf3fa; padding: 5px;"><span style="font-size: large; color: #000000;">d</span></td><td align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; background-color: #f9e1ff; padding: 5px;"><span style="font-size: large; color: #000000;">n</span></td><td align="right" style="border: 1px solid black; background-color: #ffffff; padding: 5px;"><span style="font-size: medium; color: #000000;">580</span></td></tr><tr><td align="left" style="border: 1px solid black; A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #91451f;">Gene H</span> is quite big and strong-looking, much larger than your typical fruit fly.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #006e5c;">Gene W</span></strong> is associated with the '<span style="color: #006e5c;"><i>waxy</i></span>' phenotype. MC	<p>57d5_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #03703d;">Gene K</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #03703d;"><i>kidney</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #03703d;">Gene K</span> has a body shape that is curved, similar to a kidney bean.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #783ca9;">Gene P</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #783ca9;"><i>prickly</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #783ca9;">Gene P</span> is covered with sharp bristles, giving it a spiky texture.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; MC	<p>82b3_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene J</span></strong> is related to the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>jerky</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #91451f;">Gene J</span> moves in rapid and sudden movements, displaying an unpredictable flight pattern.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #27638d;">Gene X</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #27638d;"><i>xanthic</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #27638d;">Gene X</span> has a fluorescent bright yellow coloring.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: 2px solid black;"><tr><th align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene F</span></strong> is linked with the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>fuzzy</i></span>' phenotype. MC	<p>a906_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene D</span></strong> is associated with the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>dewy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #91451f;">Gene D</span> appears moist, with its body covered in tiny droplets of water.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #006b6f;">Gene E</span></strong> is analogous to the '<span style="color: #006b6f;"><i>eery</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #006b6f;">Gene E</span> appears to have something off, crooked limbs and other twisted appendages.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse; border: 2px solid black;"><tr><th align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; background-color: #cccccc; padding: 10px;"><span style="font-size: medium; color: #000000;">Phenotype</span></th><th colspan="2" align="center" style="border: 1px solid black; MC	<p>4c92_b2bd</p> <h4>Two-Point Test Cross Problem</h4><p>A test cross is a way to explore the relationship between genes and their respective alleles. It is a useful tool for genetic mapping and deciphering the inheritance of traits. Specifically, a two-point test cross examines two (2) genes at the same time to learn about their assortment in gamete formation.</p><p>A standard two-point test cross involves crossing a heterozygous organism for both genes with an organism that is homozygous recessive for both genes</p> <p>For this problem, a test cross using a fruit fly (<i>Drosophila melanogaster</i>) heterozygous for two genes was conducted to understand their genetic interactions.</p><h6>Characteristics of Recessive Phenotypes</h6><p><ul><li><strong><span style="color: #91451f;">Gene J</span></strong> is connected with the '<span style="color: #91451f;"><i>jerky</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #91451f;">Gene J</span> moves in rapid and sudden movements, displaying an unpredictable flight pattern.</li> <li><strong><span style="color: #564abb;">Gene M</span></strong> is correlated with the '<span style="color: #564abb;"><i>mushy</i></span>' phenotype. A fruit fly that is homozygous recessive for <span style="color: #564abb;">Gene M</span> feels soft to the touch and unusually squishy, unlike the usual firmness.</li> </ul></p><table style="border-collapse: collapse;
